docs.text: 👋 Hey there! Purpose This website documents my hands-on IT lab environment, built to simulate the infrastructure of a small enterprise network. Each project is interconnected as part of one larger environment, including Active Directory, domain-joined workstations, Group Policy, file services, network segmentation, permissions management, and future infrastructure services. The goal of this lab is to move beyond isolated tutorials and build a realistic environment where each technology supports the others. Through this project, I am developing practical experience in system administration, networking, identity management, troubleshooting, and technical documentation. Another Note! As a lifelong gamer, I wanted this lab to feel like more than just a collection of servers, users, and network diagrams. The naming conventions throughout the environment are inspired by the worlds, characters, and themes found in Hideo Kojima’s games. This gives the lab a bit of personality while still keeping the structure realistic and professional. Servers, workstations, users, groups, and other resources follow a consistent naming approach, but with references that make the environment more memorable and enjoyable to build. Think of it as a small enterprise network with a little tactical espionage flair. ~ Dylan Phan

docs.text: Domain Controller Setup Hostname: Kojima-DC01 IP Address: 10.10.10.10 Windows Version: Windows Server 2022 Kojima-DC01 serves as the central domain controller for the lab, hosting Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS). It provides centralized authentication and allows domain users, computers, security groups, and other network resources to be managed from a single location. Virtual Machine Settings Initial Setup

docs.text: OpenWRT Configuration Interface VMware Network Gateway eth0 VMware NAT DHCP Client eth1 AD-SERVERNET 10.10.10.1/24 eth2 AD-WORKSTATION 10.10.20.1/24 Verifying Interfaces ip link WAN Configuration Configuring WAN interface: uci set network.wan='interface' uci set network.wan.device='eth0' uci set network.wan.proto='dhcp' By default, OpenWRT assigned eth0 to the br-lan bridge and used it as the initial LAN interface. Because this adapter was intended to provide upstream internet connectivity, it needed to be removed from the LAN bridge and configured as a DHCP-based WAN interface. Removing eth0 from br-lan: uci del_list network.@device[0].ports='eth0' ### Commiting Changes and restarting network services uci commit network /etc/init.d/network restart ### Verifying br-lan has been removed ip addr Verifying eth0 is receiving IP Address: Verifying External Connectivity Server and Workstation Network Server - eth1 Workstation - eth2

docs.text: Configuring WAN interface: uci set network.wan='interface' uci set network.wan.device='eth0' uci set network.wan.proto='dhcp' By default, OpenWRT assigned eth0 to the br-lan bridge and used it as the initial LAN interface. Because this adapter was intended to provide upstream internet connectivity, it needed to be removed from the LAN bridge and configured as a DHCP-based WAN interface. Removing eth0 from br-lan: uci del_list network.@device[0].ports='eth0' ### Commiting Changes and restarting network services uci commit network /etc/init.d/network restart ### Verifying br-lan has been removed ip addr Verifying eth0 is receiving IP Address: Verifying External Connectivity

docs.text: OpenWRT Virtual Machine Setup OpenWrt acts as the lab’s virtual router and network gateway. It provides routing, firewall control, DHCP services, and traffic separation between the server and workstation subnets, helping the environment behave more like a real small enterprise network. Preparing OpenWRT .vmdk By default, OpenWRT is downloaded as a .img file since it meant to be flashed onto network hardware. Since VMware Workstation does not natively support .img files, I needed to convert it to a .vmdk file. After some research, I decided to using qemu-tools in WSL 2.0 in order to complete the conversion. Installing Qemu Tools on Ubuntu WSL 2.0 > sudo apt update > sudo apt install qemu-utils -y ### Creating Directory in my home folder for the files > mkdir openwrt Converting .img to .vmdk > qemu-img convert -f raw -O vmdk openwrt.img openwrt.vmdk > ls ### openwrt.img openwrt.vmdk VMware Workstation Settings To prepare the environment for OpenWrt, I first configured VMware’s virtual networks to act as the backbone of the lab. These networks allow the router, servers, and workstations to communicate while keeping each subnet properly separated. VMware Virtual Networks AD-SERVERNET — 10.10.10.0/24 Host-only VMware network Used by domain controllers, file servers, and other infrastructure servers VMware DHCP disabled IP addressing and network services are managed through OpenWrt AD-WORKSTATION — 10.10.20.0/24 Host-only VMware network Used by domain-joined workstations and client devices VMware DHCP disabled OpenWrt provides DHCP leases and routing for connected workstations WAN Connected to VMware’s NAT-enabled network DHCP enabled Allows the OpenWrt WAN interface to automatically receive an IP address Provides internet access to the internal server and workstation networks through OpenWrt OpenWRT Virtual Machine Settings Hard Disk: With the converted .vmdk file, we can attach the boot drive to the virtual hard disk image to boot into Openwrt. Network Adapter: Connected to VMware’s NAT-enabled network. This interface receives an IP address through DHCP and provides the OpenWrt router with internet access. Network Adapter 2: Connected to the AD-SERVERNET host-only network. This interface serves as the default gateway for domain controllers, file servers, and other infrastructure servers on the 10.10.10.0/24 subnet. Network Adapter 3: Connected to the AD-WORKSTATION host-only network. This interface provides routing and DHCP services for domain-joined workstations on the 10.10.20.0/24 subnet.
docs.title: OpenWRT VM

After some research, I decided to using qemu-tools in WSL 2.0 in order to complete the conversion. This interface receives an IP address through DHCP and provides the OpenWrt router with internet access. Network Adapter 2: Connected to the AD-SERVERNET host-only network. This interface serves as the default gateway for domain controllers, file servers, and other infrastructure servers on the 10.10.10.0/24 subnet. Network Adapter 3: Connected to the AD-WORKSTATION host-only network. This interface provides routing and DHCP services for domain-joined workstations on the 10.10.20.0/24 subnet.

docs.text: Networking Overview Server Network The server network in this lab will be used to house the various servers that will host critical domain resources and services. Server Role IP Address Kojima-DC01 Domain Controller / DNS 10.10.10.10 Kojima-FS01 File Server 10.10.10.20 Kojima-APPS01 Ubuntu server that will be used to serve Docker container applications to other machines on the network. 10.10.10.30 Workstation Network This network will house the Windows 11 clients that will be accessing services and resources from the server network Server Role IP Address Kojima-WS01 (Admin Machine) This machine will be used by a "IT Administrator." They should have the ability to create and manage domain resources and services DHCP Kojima-WS02 (Standard Machine) This is for a standard enterprise user that will not have administrator privileges DHCP
docs.title: Network Overview
